text:
Coordination complexes containing a $d^6$ $metal$ center such as Ru(II), Os(II), and Ir(III) are ubiquitously found in many applications related to lighting, solar energy conversion and photocatalysis. Realizing similar photophysical properties in abundant first row transition metals has remained an open question. While most recent investigations have concentrated on Fe(II), we now demonstrate that the isoelectronic Cr(0) metal center allows for much longer-lived excited states as well as enhanced photoluminescence quantum yields relative to all previously reported first row $d^6$ complexes.

Cr(0) complexes were prepared by strategic molecular design and their photophysics as the metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) excited state was studied and characterized. Unexpectedly, Cr(0) complexes showed luminescence within a few percent of maximum efficiency compared to known benchmark compounds composed of an osmium(II) centre and showed robust photophysical properties. They proved to act as high power reductants in photoredox catalysis by enabling challenging reductions to proceed upon low energy (red light) irradiation. In this work, by strategic molecular design, we are able to bypass the limitations of 3d metals and show the capabilities of earth abundant elements in terms of photophysics and catalysis.
